1 NEM Total Monthly Transfers — Overall Trend
matplotlib · 2-year rolling window · annotated events
Key observation: The market grew steadily from 2016 to early 2018, then entered a downturn through 2019. COVID caused a mild -3.9% contraction. April 2021 hit the all-time peak at 276k (post-lockdown pent-up demand + aggressive retailer promotions). The July 2022 energy price shock triggered a sharp short-term spike followed by elevated volatility. Since 2023 the market has stabilised at a 160–180k/month plateau, suggesting maturity.
2 Regional Breakdown — NSW · QLD · SA · VIC
matplotlib · 4-panel · 12-month rolling average overlay
VIC + NSW together account for ~75% of all NEM transfers. QLD saw a notable 2017–2018 surge (market deregulation wave) that has since moderated. SA is the smallest market by volume but shows proportionally competitive churn. All regions display synchronised seasonality — peaking each July.
3 Seasonality Analysis
matplotlib · NEM total bar + normalised regional index
Strong, consistent seasonality exists across all regions. July is the undisputed peak (+19% above annual mean) — Australian winter electricity bills trigger comparison-shopping and switching behaviour. January and December form the trough (-13% and -8% respectively) due to summer holidays. VIC shows the most pronounced July spike (index ~130), reflecting its highly competitive retail market.
4 Annualised Churn Rate & Regional Market Share
VIC consistently leads with an avg 24.8% annual churn — 10 pp above QLD. All regions spiked in mid-2021 (up to ~35% in VIC). Post-2022 churn rates have converged downward, signalling reduced competitive intensity as the market matures.
VIC and NSW shares are roughly equal (~38% each) and stable. QLD's share has drifted down from ~20% (2018) to ~17% (2025), reflecting slower growth relative to other states. SA holds a steady ~8%.
5 Monthly Transfers Heatmap — Month × Year
matplotlib · annotated cell values
Two structural patterns are immediately visible: Vertical (month axis) — the July row is the darkest in every single year, confirming rock-solid seasonality. Horizontal (year axis) — 2018 and 2021–2022 columns are notably warmer, mapping to the two major market activity cycles. The April 2021 cell (276k) is a clear outlier and should be treated as a one-time structural break in trend analysis.
6 Year-over-Year Change
matplotlib · green = growth · red = decline
Three distinct phases: (1) 2017–2018 growth (+10–35% YoY, market expansion); (2) 2019–2020 contraction (market saturation + COVID, sustained red bars); (3) 2021 spike and 2022 reversal (+134% in Apr-21, then -52% in Jul-22 on base effects + retailer exits); (4) 2023–2025 normalisation — alternating ±10–25%, no clear directional trend.
7 SARIMA 12-Month Forecast — Rolling Average Outlook
Methodology: Seasonal ARIMA fitted independently per region using auto_arima (AIC-optimised, seasonal period m=12, one seasonal difference). The rolling average line extends into forecast territory as a dashed line. Shaded bands show 80% (darker) and 95% (lighter) confidence intervals. The dotted vertical line marks the boundary between observed and forecast data.
All four regions forecast a July 2026 seasonal peak — consistent with the historical pattern. VIC and NSW show the widest confidence bands, reflecting higher month-to-month volatility. QLD's Oct-26 forecast spike reflects its historically elevated spring switching activity. Forecasts should be treated as indicative; unannounced policy or tariff changes could shift outcomes materially.

10 Key Findings & Implications

📈 Market Has Reached a Mature Plateau

Monthly volumes have hovered around 160–180k since 2023, with YoY growth near zero (+1.0%). The market is saturating — competitive focus is shifting from acquisition to retention of existing customers.

❄️ July Seasonality Is Rock-Solid

July has been the single highest month in every one of the 9 years covered. Winter electricity bills consistently drive comparison-shopping. Retailers should front-load retention campaigns to May–June, before customers have already decided to switch.

⚡ Energy Price Shocks Dwarf COVID Impact

COVID caused only a -3.9% dip. The 2022 energy price shock generated ±50% YoY swings and months of elevated volatility. Price events are the dominant driver of switching behaviour — far more powerful than economic downturns.

🗺 VIC Is the Leading Indicator Market

At 24.8% avg annual churn, VIC leads all regions by ~5–10 pp. VIC trends tend to precede similar movements in NSW and SA by 1–2 months, making it a useful early-warning indicator for NEM-wide switching activity.

⚠️ April 2021 Is a Structural Outlier

The 276k peak is a one-time confluence of post-lockdown demand release and retailer promotional surges — not a sustainable demand level. Trend models and forecasts should exclude or adjust this point to avoid upward bias.

📉 QLD Share Is Gradually Declining

QLD's share of NEM transfers has fallen from ~20% (2018) to ~17% (2025). Slower deregulation progress and lower consumer engagement mean QLD is growing the total market more slowly than VIC and NSW.

Data Source: AEMO MSATS Monthly Retail Transfer Statistical Reports  |  Analysis Period: 2016-01 – 2026-04 (124 months)  |  Regions: NSW, QLD, SA, VIC  |  Generated May 2026
